topic: Partial identification of dynamic treatment effects under non-Markov assignments and imperfect compliance

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize observable sufficient conditions under which retained non-Markov assignment history strictly tightens sharp bounds for two-stage dynamic IV effects under imperfect compliance.

source_refs.claim: They characterize ATE and ATT identified sets under covariate-adaptive randomization with imperfect compliance, but their assignment strata are pre-treatment covariates rather than post-baseline memory labels.
source_refs.author: Bugni, Gao, Obradovic, and Velez

source_refs.claim: Autobounds gives generic sharp bounds for discrete causal queries, but not an analytic criterion for when assignment memory creates strict endpoint gain.
source_refs.author: Duarte, Finkelstein, Knox, Mummolo, and Shpitser
source_refs.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association

source_refs.claim: Dynamic LATEs are identified under one-sided noncompliance plus an added cross-period effect-compliance independence condition; the residual partial-ID regime is not resolved.
source_refs.author: Sojitra and Syrgkanis

source_refs.excerpt: Sharpen Conjecture 2 into observable sufficient conditions for strict gain
post-baseline randomized encouragement can become a principal-stratum splitter

open_problems.why_unsolved: The web literature pins down three surrounding pieces: static imperfect-compliance CAR bounds, generic discrete sharp-bound computation, and dynamic LATE point identification under extra cross-period restrictions. The v2 ThmSmith proposal supplies a two-stage memory-splitting formula and witness, but its reviewer explicitly left open the observable strict-gain boundary, especially the split between coordinate informativeness of the pure never-taker branch and joint compatibility restrictions.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A necessary-and-sufficient LP-face or observable-inequality theorem for strict memory gain, stated in terms of the observed law and known assignment probabilities.

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize when local dynamic IV partial-identification certificates are globally pasteable into one full-history latent compliance table.

source_refs.claim: They derive a Bellman equation and IV-optimal DTR objects under partial identification, but the object is regime optimization rather than compatibility of local IV certificates.
source_refs.author: Chen and Zhang
source_refs.venue: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series B

source_refs.claim: Han recovers optimal dynamic regimes through sharp partial welfare orderings under IVs, not through a theorem describing the global image of local compliance polytopes.
source_refs.author: Han
source_refs.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association

source_refs.claim: Generic discrete sharp-bound machinery can encode the full problem but does not isolate a reusable pasteability or non-pasteability criterion for dynamic IV certificates.
source_refs.author: Duarte, Finkelstein, Knox, Mummolo, and Shpitser
source_refs.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association

source_refs.excerpt: globally pasteable if there exists a full-history feasible

source_refs.where: What a field-tier repair would have to do
source_refs.excerpt: a precise non-pasting theorem for local dynamic IV certificates with a sharp primal/dual witness

open_problems.why_unsolved: Dynamic IV regime papers use Bellman or welfare-ordering objects, while generic discrete-bound methods optimize over the full latent table without exposing when locally valid IV subproblems can be realized jointly. The v1 proposal defined product-local certificates, but the derivation was downgraded because it did not deliver a novel global compatibility theorem beyond standard finite LP sharpness.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A theorem describing the image of the global compatibility map inside the product of local IV polytopes, with a primal feasible construction and a dual or Farkas certificate for non-pasteability.

open_problems.open_problem: Extend assignment-memory mixture splitting to bounded-defier or two-sided compliance models where no observed history reveals a pure principal stratum.

source_refs.claim: The canonical IV principal-strata framework uses monotonicity to interpret the LATE as a complier effect in a static decision.
source_refs.author: Angrist, Imbens, and Rubin
source_refs.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association

source_refs.claim: Static LATE assumptions imply testable conditional inequalities, showing that compliance restrictions have observable content even before dynamic history enters.
source_refs.author: Mourifie and Wan

source_refs.claim: Their dynamic LATE framework is built around one-sided noncompliance and added cross-period assumptions, leaving the two-sided or bounded-defier partial-ID case open.
source_refs.author: Sojitra and Syrgkanis

source_refs.excerpt: same memory mechanism may survive with bounded defiers rather than monotonicity

open_problems.why_unsolved: The accepted v2 mechanism depends on one-sided first-stage compliance to turn one history into a pure never-taker branch and another into a complier-never-taker mixture. Once defiers or two-sided compliance are admitted, every observed branch may be a multi-stratum mixture, so the subtraction formula and strict-gain diagnostic no longer apply directly.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A sharp interval or sensitivity theorem indexed by bounded defier mass, with observable inequality restrictions and a regime statement for when retained assignment memory still tightens the bounds.

open_problems.open_problem: Bound long-run dynamic treatment effects in eligibility and exit models when assigned treatment timing is randomized or as-good-as-random but realized take-up is imperfect.

source_refs.claim: They study long-run dynamic effects when treatment may start at different times and individuals must remain eligible, but without an IV principal-strata noncompliance layer.
source_refs.author: van den Berg and Vikstrom

source_refs.claim: The duration-treatment framework handles treatment timing and exit risks, giving the dynamic selection structure a later imperfect-compliance bound would need to respect.
source_refs.author: Abbring and van den Berg

source_refs.claim: Balke-Pearl gives tight static nonparametric bounds for randomized assignment with imperfect compliance, but not dynamic eligibility and exit histories.
source_refs.author: Balke and Pearl
source_refs.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association

open_problems.why_unsolved: Dynamic eligibility creates competing risks: subjects may exit before later treatment opportunities and compliance types are selected by survival in the risk set. Existing dynamic-timing work typically assumes treatment timing is unconfounded conditional on eligibility history, while static IV bounds do not encode delayed compliers, never-takers, and exit-before-compliance types.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: Sharp bounds for effects of assignment timing and actual treatment timing, with latent types for delayed compliance and exit-before-compliance and a theorem separating direct assignment effects from total treatment-timing effects where possible.

open_problems.open_problem: Develop a staggered event-study or DID decomposition for dynamic complier effects under imperfect compliance and heterogeneous treatment timing.

source_refs.claim: They show conventional dynamic TWFE event-study coefficients can be contaminated by heterogeneous cohort-specific effects.
source_refs.author: Sun and Abraham

source_refs.claim: They identify group-time treatment effects in multiple-period DID under conditional parallel trends, without an imperfect-compliance principal-strata layer.
source_refs.author: Callaway and Sant'Anna

source_refs.claim: They show TWFE estimators can be non-convex averages of heterogeneous treatment effects, motivating explicit weight decompositions.
source_refs.author: de Chaisemartin and D'Haultfoeuille

source_refs.claim: They derive robust and efficient event-study estimators for staggered adoption, but the target is not a latent dynamic complier effect.
source_refs.author: Borusyak, Jaravel, and Spiess

open_problems.why_unsolved: Modern event-study work has clarified heterogeneous timing and negative-weight pathologies for adoption effects, while IV/LATE work localizes effects to compliance strata. Combining them is not a mechanical overlay: the cohort-time weights would have to track latent history-specific compliers, and imperfect take-up can make the received-treatment event time differ from the assigned-adoption event time.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A decomposition theorem for IV-DID or event-study estimands into cohort-time-history complier effects, with sharp bounds or convex-weight conditions when exact decomposition fails.

literature_map:
Imbens and Angrist (1994) and Angrist, Imbens, and Rubin (1996) define the static LATE and compliance-type benchmark.
Balke and Pearl (1997) and Swanson, Hernan, Miller, Robins, and Richardson (2018) give the static binary-IV partial-identification and bounds vocabulary for imperfect compliance.
Manski and Pepper (2000) show how monotone instrumental-variable and response restrictions tighten static treatment-response bounds.
Bugni, Gao, Obradovic, and Velez (2024) characterize ATE/ATT identified sets under covariate-adaptive randomization and imperfect compliance, but in static pre-treatment strata.
Duarte, Finkelstein, Knox, Mummolo, and Shpitser (2024) provide generic automated sharp bounds for discrete causal problems; the gap is analytic structure and diagnostics in dynamic IV submodels.
Sojitra and Syrgkanis (2024) identify Dynamic LATEs under one-sided noncompliance plus additional cross-period effect-compliance independence.
Michael, Cui, Lorch, and Tchetgen Tchetgen (2024) identify time-varying-IV marginal structural mean model parameters under compliance-type restrictions, but not nonparametric sharp intervals.
Chen and Zhang (2023) and Han (2023) push IVs into optimal dynamic regimes via Bellman or welfare-ordering objects, not fixed-target assignment-memory sharp bounds.
Han (2021) and dynamic panel partial-ID papers show that full treatment histories create partial identification even before imperfect compliance is added.
van den Berg and Vikstrom (2022) and Abbring and van den Berg (2003) give dynamic eligibility and duration structures where imperfect take-up would add principal-strata ambiguity.
Sun and Abraham (2021), Callaway and Sant'Anna (2021), de Chaisemartin and D'Haultfoeuille (2020), and Borusyak, Jaravel, and Spiess (2024) solve many staggered event-study weighting problems without a compliance layer.
The central tension is that dynamic designs require history-specific causal paths, while IV compliance logic localizes effects to latent path-specific compliers.
The live frontier is sharp, computable partial identification for full-history dynamic IV models, with interpretable conditions that avoid Markov or strong cross-period point-ID shortcuts.
prior_proposal_map:
Walked parent bank README first: ThmSmith/doc/formalization/_bank/candidates/pid_dynamic_iv_compliance_v2/README.md marks v2 accepted and points to the proposal, derivation, and reviews.
Mined v2 proposal and reviews: yielded the assignment-memory mixture-splitting formula, product-envelope failure, and reviewer-prescribed strict-gain characterization gap.
Used v2 reviewer JSON angle1_v3 for the explicit high-effort strengthening path: observable sufficient conditions separating B_N informativeness from J_N compatibility.
Used v2 Stage 0.5 attempt2 for broader compatibility/general memory-gain and LP-stability gaps; kept only the compatibility gap in the filtered output.
Mined downgraded v1 README and proposal: yielded the post-baseline full-history separation and local-certificate non-pasteability gaps.
Checked rejected v2 angle0: the Honest scope flags explicitly identify bounded-defier or monotonicity-violating compliance as unworked scope.
No active v3 proposal exists yet; the active qid directory only contained v3 state/pipeline scaffolding at scan time.
